lda_top_words: household, child, health, woman, diversity, nutrition, dietary, policy, womens, population
note: Top words (household, child, woman, nutrition, dietary) describe food insecurity outcomes. Used as the dependent variable throughout. Future work: add stunting_pct_children or dietary diversity as secondary outcome measures.

lda_top_words: loss, postharvest, storage, grain, chain, supply, quality, value, fruit, vegetable
note: In Model F (HC3): b=-0.456, p=0.236 (n.s.). IMPORTANT: the coefficient is negative — more recorded loss is associated with LOWER undernourishment in the N=80 sample. This is counter-intuitive and likely reflects sample composition: countries with detailed loss accounting (APHLIS/FBS) are predominantly middle-income countries with lower undernourishment. In Model B (full N=158) the result is b=0.022, p=0.921 (n.s.). UPGRADE from previous version: now uses real APHLIS + FAO FBS country-level data (149 unique values, 159 countries) rather than 4 continental averages.

lda_top_words: climate, change, adaptation, impact, risk, strategy, water, farmer, vulnerability, review
note: Annual average precipitation (b=1.049) is not significant (p≈0.12) and has the wrong sign. Annual average rainfall is a poor proxy for climate risk — variability (coefficient of variation) matters more than the average. This does not mean climate is unimportant; it means this cross-sectional dataset cannot detect the effect with this proxy.

lda_top_words: development, system, policy, sustainable, research, agricultural, framework, agriculture, social, digital
note: WGI political stability (b=-0.302, p≈0.73) is absorbed by GDP per capita. This is a well-known collinearity in cross-country data — rich countries have both better governance and lower undernourishment. Panel data with country fixed effects would resolve this.

lda_top_words: soil, water, crop, land, production, global, environmental, fertiliser, potential, organic
note: Fertiliser use confirmed: b=-1.339, p=0.024 (**) in Model A, significant in 5/7 robustness specs. Cereal yield is always negative (right direction) but never independently significant due to severe multicollinearity with fertiliser (VIF > 100). HC3 robust standard errors are used throughout to account for heteroskedasticity in the residuals.

lda_top_words: household, farmer, agricultural, farm, smallholder, income, access, rural, result, financial
proxy_variable:
poverty_headcount_pct_215 (Model F, *, HC3)
mobile_subscriptions_per_100 (Model F, n.s., HC3)
value_chain_finance_score (Model D, n.s., HC3)
note:
CORE CONTRIBUTION (partial evidence). NLP-discovered proxies show directional support with HC3-corrected standard errors:
  1. poverty_headcount_pct_215: b=0.233, p=0.081 (*, HC3) — marginal significance; bootstrap 95% CI excludes zero.
  2. mobile_subscriptions_per_100: b=-0.042, p=0.112 (n.s., HC3) — correct direction but CI crosses zero; n.s. with robust SEs.
  3. value_chain_finance_score: b=-0.097, p=0.275 (n.s., HC3) in Model D — n.s. with current Findex variables.
HC3 SEs are more conservative than standard OLS. Partial confirmation is the honest finding given N=80 and VIF > 100.

lda_top_words: system, global, production, sustainable, emission, development, technology, future, agriculture, energy
note: Technology and sustainability themes were not independently tested because GDP per capita already captures broad development (including digital infrastructure). Adding internet_users_pct introduces collinearity with GDP. mobile_subscriptions_per_100 in Model F partially captures this theme.

lda_top_words: land, forest, oil, expansion, agricultural, biodiversity, palm, deforestation, soil, crop
note: arable_land_pct is a static proxy; the LDA topic is about DYNAMIC land use change (deforestation, palm oil). Direction is correct (b=-0.067: more arable land → less undernourishment) but not consistently significant (confirmed in 4/7 specs).

lda_top_words: yield, crop, global, soil, maize, production, plant, climate, area, disease
note: Cereal yield is consistently negative (b=-0.783: higher yield → less undernourishment) but never independently significant due to collinearity with fertiliser (VIF > 100). In Model F cereal_yield IS significant (p=0.010, ***) when fertiliser loses power — confirming that yield matters, but it cannot be separated from inputs in multivariate OLS.
